sectionName: Biological & Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems

questions.passage: Researchers measured blood metabolites after an overnight fast and after a 72-hour fast. Blood glucose decreased modestly, free fatty acids increased, and ketone bodies increased substantially after 72 hours. Liver glycogen content was low after 72 hours, while expression of enzymes involved in beta-oxidation and ketogenesis was elevated. Red blood cells continued to require glucose because they lack mitochondria.
questions.stem: Which metabolite increases most after 72 hours of fasting?

explanations.B: Correct. Ketone bodies rise from a very low value to a high value after prolonged fasting, representing the largest increase in the figure.
explanations.D: Not all metabolites decrease; fatty acids and ketones rise.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Relative blood glucose, free fatty acids, ketone bodies, and liver glycogen after overnight versus 72-hour fasting.

questions.stem: Why do ketone bodies increase during prolonged fasting?

choices.C: The liver converts fatty-acid-derived acetyl-CoA into ketone bodies when carbohydrate availability is limited
choices.A: Red blood cells make ketones from hemoglobin
choices.B: Ketones are produced only from DNA replication
choices.D: Ketogenesis requires high liver glycogen stores

explanations.C: Correct. During prolonged fasting, beta-oxidation produces acetyl-CoA in liver, and limited carbohydrate availability favors ketogenesis.
explanations.A: Red blood cells lack mitochondria and do not perform ketogenesis.
explanations.B: DNA replication is not the source of ketones.
explanations.D: Liver glycogen is low during prolonged fasting.

questions.stem: Why do red blood cells continue to require glucose?

choices.A: They lack mitochondria and cannot rely on oxidative metabolism of fatty acids or ketones
choices.B: They contain more mitochondria than muscle cells
choices.C: They use the TCA cycle as their only ATP source

explanations.A: Correct. Mature red blood cells lack mitochondria, so they depend on glycolysis for ATP and require glucose.
explanations.C: They cannot run the mitochondrial TCA cycle.
explanations.D: Plasma proteins are mainly synthesized by the liver, not RBCs.

questions.stem: What happens to liver glycogen during the 72-hour fast?

choices.C: It becomes the main source of ketone bodies

explanations.D: Correct. The figure shows liver glycogen falling from 78 to 12 relative units after 72 hours of fasting.
explanations.A: The direction is downward, not upward.
explanations.C: Ketone bodies are produced primarily from fatty-acid-derived acetyl-CoA, not glycogen.

questions.stem: Which hormonal pattern would best support the 72-hour fasting state?

choices.B: High insulin and high leptin after a meal
choices.D: Low glucagon and high glycogen synthesis

explanations.C: Correct. Prolonged fasting is associated with low insulin, high glucagon, lipolysis, hepatic glucose output, and ketogenesis.
explanations.A: This is the fed-state pattern, not prolonged fasting.
explanations.B: High insulin after a meal would suppress ketogenesis.
explanations.D: Low glucagon and high glycogen synthesis are inconsistent with fasting.

questions.passage: A mitochondrial physiology lab treated cells with Compound Z, an inhibitor of Complex IV of the electron transport chain. Oxygen consumption fell rapidly, ATP production decreased, and lactate release increased. Adding an uncoupler in untreated cells increased oxygen consumption while lowering ATP yield, but adding the uncoupler after Compound Z did not restore oxygen consumption. The researchers concluded that Compound Z blocks electron transfer to oxygen rather than merely dissipating the proton gradient.
questions.stem: What is the primary effect of Compound Z?

choices.D: It acts exactly like an uncoupler alone

explanations.B: Correct. Compound Z lowers oxygen consumption from 100 to 18 relative units, consistent with blocking electron transfer to oxygen at Complex IV.
explanations.A: ATP production decreases rather than increases.
explanations.D: The uncoupler alone increases oxygen consumption, unlike Compound Z.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Oxygen consumption, ATP production, and lactate release in cells exposed to Complex IV inhibitor Compound Z and/or an uncoupler.

questions.stem: Why does lactate release increase after Compound Z treatment?

choices.D: Cells rely more on glycolysis when oxidative phosphorylation is impaired
choices.A: Cells stop regenerating NAD+ in glycolysis
choices.B: Complex IV directly converts lactate into DNA
choices.C: Lactate is produced only by mitochondria

explanations.D: Correct. When ETC function and ATP production fall, cells increase glycolytic flux. Lactate formation helps regenerate NAD+ for glycolysis.
explanations.A: Lactate production helps regenerate NAD+ rather than stopping it.
explanations.B: Complex IV transfers electrons to oxygen; it does not convert lactate into DNA.
explanations.C: Lactate is produced in the cytosol, not only by mitochondria.

questions.stem: How does the uncoupler condition differ from Compound Z alone?

choices.A: The uncoupler increases oxygen consumption while lowering ATP yield
choices.B: The uncoupler blocks Complex IV completely
choices.C: The uncoupler prevents all electron flow
choices.D: The uncoupler increases ATP production above control

explanations.A: Correct. Uncouplers dissipate the proton gradient, causing the ETC to run faster and consume more oxygen while ATP synthesis becomes inefficient.
explanations.B: The high oxygen consumption shows Complex IV is not blocked by the uncoupler alone.
explanations.C: Electron flow increases rather than stops with uncoupling.
explanations.D: ATP production decreases relative to control.

questions.stem: Why does adding uncoupler after Compound Z fail to restore oxygen consumption?

choices.B: Electron transfer to oxygen is blocked downstream, so dissipating the gradient cannot drive flow
choices.A: The uncoupler becomes a ribosome inhibitor
choices.C: Compound Z increases oxygen consumption too strongly
choices.D: The proton gradient is the only possible site of ETC control

explanations.B: Correct. If Complex IV is blocked, electrons cannot be passed efficiently to oxygen. Removing the proton gradient cannot overcome a downstream block in electron transfer.
explanations.A: The passage does not describe ribosome inhibition.
explanations.D: The data show control can occur at Complex IV as well as the gradient.

questions.stem: Which process directly uses the proton gradient to synthesize ATP?

explanations.C: Correct. ATP synthase harnesses the proton-motive force generated by the ETC to phosphorylate ADP to ATP.
explanations.D: Hemoglobin binds oxygen but does not synthesize ATP.
